summary: Gruber reviews Apple's Spring Loaded event, covering Apple Card Family's co-ownership innovation, Apple Podcasts subscriptions done right, AirTags as simple trackers, a new Apple TV remote that fixes years of bad design, colorful M1 iMacs, and M1 iPad Pros. He praises the new Apple TV remote while calling out the baffling omission of Find My support in a remote debuting alongside AirTags. The colorful iMacs get enthusiastic praise for their thinness and cheerful design. He pushes back on complaints about iPad Pro getting the M1 chip, noting it's just a name and doesn't make running macOS any more likely. He also notes Apple's conspicuous silence on iOS 14.5's anti-tracking features.
key_points: Apple Card Family's co-ownership model where there's no primary cardholder is a genuine innovation, not just playing catch-up on shared credit cards

The old Siri Remote was a notoriously bad design that Apple inexplicably shipped for five years, and the new remote's lack of Find My support alongside AirTags suggests Apple's siloed organizational culture
Apple Podcasts subscriptions are done right because they layer on top of the existing open RSS ecosystem rather than replacing it
The M1 in iPad Pro is functionally an A14X by another name, and the branding alone doesn't make running macOS any more likely or technically feasible
The new iMacs' extreme thinness and vibrant colors are a design statement worth making, and dismissing them is like asking 'who cares what color plastic your computer is' twenty years ago
money_quotes: These new iMacs are less than 1mm thicker than a goddamned Apple Watch.
If you don't think that's cool and that cool is something Apple should aspire to in its design and engineering, I have no idea why you're reading anything I write.
It's like the black trackpad 'Siri Remote' never happened.
The inexplicable part isn't that Apple shipped it in the first place, but that they stuck with it for over five years.
These new subscriptions feel less like steering Apple Podcasts in the proprietary direction of Apple News, and more like adding built-in exclusive website subscriptions to Safari.
There seem to be a lot of people who want to like iPads but who don't like iPadOS or iPad apps.
It's like the Mac of streaming boxes. You either see it as worth the premium, or you think everyone who buys one is the proverbial fool being parted from their money.
I can only chalk this up to Apple's siloed culture — perhaps the team designing the new remote had no idea what the AirTag/Find My teams were up to.
key_insight: Apple's Spring Loaded event was strongest where products had clear, simple purposes and weakest where organizational silos prevented obvious integrations like Find My in the new Apple TV remote.
